comments.text: Typo: [Ha] is loading J. A. Haight's PhD Thesis instead of L. Habseiger's paper. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

In the case $A!B! = C!$, there has been significant improvements: The equation has been studied by Caldwell for $C \le 10^6$ in [Ca94]. Also, Erd¨os in [Er93] proved $C − B ≤ 5 \log \log C$ , which was improved further to $C−B ≤ (1/ \log 2+o(1)) \log \log C$ by Bhat and Ramachandra in this paper. Laurent Habseiger in his paper has checked the conjecture for $B ≤ 10^{3000}$ and give better explicit bounds such as\[C −B ≤ \frac{\log \log(B+1)}{\log 2} − 0.8803.\][Ca94]: C. Caldwell, The diophantine equation A!B! = C!, J. Recreat. Math. 26 (1994), 128–133. [Er93]: P. Erd¨os, A consequence of a factorial equation, Amer. Math. Monthly 100 (1993), 407–408. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Extremely general question, which speaks to its difficulty. It is essentially equivalent to the question of whether there are any numbers other than 9 and 10, which are not Jordan-Pólya numbers, but such that their factorials are products of smaller factorials. There are probably not.
